turns.content: I've read about the frunk crate and its ability to represent heterogeneous lists in Rust, but I still don't fully grasp how it actually works under the hood compared to standard Vec implementations.

The core difference lies in how types are handled at compile time. While a standard Vec<T> requires all elements to be of the same type, frunk allows you to construct lists where each element can have a different type by leveraging Rust's generic constraints and tuple-like structures. Instead of using dynamic allocation like Vec does with heap memory, HList represents data as a sequence of values stored directly in the stack, which means it is fully static and doesn't allocate at runtime.
That makes sense regarding storage, but I'm confused about how you actually define such a list when building a configuration object or passing arguments to a function that expects mixed types.

You define an HList by specifying each type explicitly in the generic parameters of your tuple-like structure. For example, you might declare a struct with fields named config_name and config_value where config_name is String and config_value is Option<i32>. The frunk crate provides utilities to manipulate these structures, such as functions that iterate over them while preserving type safety without needing runtime checks.
So when I call a function expecting an HList, does it automatically know which element is which based on position alone?

Not exactly. Position alone isn't enough because Rust's type system doesn't infer order from indices in the same way dynamic languages do. Instead, you use named fields or positional parameters with explicit type annotations so that each slot in your HList corresponds to a specific type like String or i32. This ensures that when you construct an instance of your HList struct, every field must match its declared generic type.
I see now about the named fields, but I'm wondering how this compares to using serde_json::Value for serialization since that's what we typically use for heterogeneous data in JSON contexts.

While serde_json::Value is flexible and supports arbitrary types at runtime through dynamic typing, it lacks the compile-time guarantees you get with frunk. HList ensures that your configuration schema is validated before execution because each type must match its declaration in the code. In contrast, serde_json::Value allows any value to be stored without strict typing checks until deserialization occurs.
